attributes.stem:
A 42-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic reporting increased conflict with her teenage children over the past three months. She describes becoming disproportionately angry with them over minor issues such as unmade beds or unwashed dishes. She works as a senior accountant and reveals that her workplace has become increasingly stressful due to an overbearing new manager who frequently criticises her work. She feels unable to express her frustrations at work as she fears losing her job.

She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears distressed with vital signs within normal limits. She becomes tearful when discussing her home situation, expressing guilt about her reactions towards her children.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following ego defence mechanisms best describes this patient's psychological process?
attributes.explanation:
This case illustrates the classic ego defence mechanism of displacement, where the patient redirects her anger and frustration from her workplace (the original source) onto her children (a safer target). Unable to express her feelings towards her manager due to fear of consequences, she unconsciously shifts these emotions to a less threatening situation at home.

Reaction formation involves converting unacceptable feelings into their opposite, such as expressing love for someone you actually dislike. This is not demonstrated in this case as the patient is not converting her anger into an opposite emotion.

Projection involves attributing one's own unacceptable thoughts or feelings to others. In this scenario, the patient is not attributing her own feelings to others but rather redirecting them to different targets.

Sublimation is the transformation of unacceptable impulses into socially acceptable activities, such as channeling aggressive urges into competitive sports. The patient is not demonstrating this type of constructive redirection.

Intellectualisation involves dealing with emotional conflict by focusing on abstract thinking or generalisations to avoid the associated feelings. The patient is clearly experiencing and expressing her emotions rather than attempting to rationalize them away.

Understanding defence mechanisms is crucial for clinicians as it helps identify patterns of maladaptive coping. In this case, recognising displacement could help guide therapy towards developing more adaptive ways of managing workplace stress and expressing emotions appropriately.
learningPoints: Displacement is an ego defence mechanism where emotions or impulses are redirected from their original target to a less threatening substitute.
Displacement commonly manifests as redirected anger or frustration towards safer targets, often family members or subordinates.
Recognition of defence mechanisms helps clinicians understand maladaptive coping patterns and guide therapeutic interventions.
references: Sadock BJ, Sadock VA, Ruiz P. Kaplan and Sadock's comprehensive textbook of psychiatry. lippincott Williams & wilkins; 2017 May 11.
Bailey R, Pico J. Defense Mechanisms. In: StatPearls [online]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing. [Updated 2023 May 22]. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK559106/
